title: Cell potential and ΔG° = −nFE°
definition: The **standard cell potential** is the voltage a galvanic cell drives at standard conditions: $E^\circ_\text{cell} = E^\circ_\text{cathode} - E^\circ_\text{anode}$, read straight off the table of standard reduction potentials (see [[standard-reduction-potential]]). It is **intensive** — it does *not* scale when you multiply the equation through, because it is energy *per unit charge*. A **positive** $E^\circ_\text{cell}$ means the reaction is spontaneous as written; the two are linked by $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$, where $n$ is the moles of electrons (see [[electron-ledger]]) and $F$ the Faraday constant. So $E^\circ_\text{cell} > 0 \Leftrightarrow \Delta G^\circ < 0$: the cell runs on its own and can do electrical work. Away from standard conditions the Nernst equation corrects $E$. The relation is exact inside the standard-state model; $E^\circ$ is a sourced measured value.
